hypothesis: Because `gated` beat the matched `topk` control at `K=96`, the next single question is whether that advantage extends one more step down the support ladder; testing `ARCHITECTURE=gated, K=64, EF=12, OPTIMIZER=signum, LR=1.6e-3` will show whether `gated` is broadly stronger across high-K frontier points or only wins near the very top end.
summary: Round 36 changed the picture: `gated@96` is not just viable, it improved the frontier over the existing `topk@96` control, so the family has earned one more K-mapping round. Keep the anchored recipe fixed and step only `K` from `96` to `64`. This directly answers whether the `gated` advantage generalizes to the next adjacent frontier point. Read the full curve, not just the endpoint: if training remains smooth and finishes below the `topk@64` control (`0.1992608`), `gated` is a real high-K mainline candidate; if it drops back behind `topk`, then the family likely helps only in the upper-K regime.
self_review: This stays on the current family, keeps EF fixed at 12, and changes exactly one main variable: `K`. It follows the prescribed ordering because recipe tuning was already exhausted before K moves began, and `K=64` is the highest-value next probe after the successful `K=96` result.
notes_to_memory: Round 36 established that gated generalizes beyond K=128 by beating the matched topk control at K=96.
Round 37 should probe the next adjacent K point, gated@64 with the same signum/LR anchor, to test whether the advantage persists deeper into the frontier.
next_hypotheses: If `gated@64` also beats `topk@64`, continue one more downward K probe because gated is shaping up as the stronger mainline family across multiple frontier points.
If `gated@64` falls back behind `topk@64`, treat gated as a high-K specialist and preserve it mainly for K>=96.
